SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by a systematic experimental evaluation: multiple agent variants (FF A3C, LSTM A3C, Nav A3C and several auxiliary-task augmentations) are compared across five DeepMind Lab mazes, with ablations (depth reg vs. classification, LSTM placement) and diagnostics such as position decoding and latency-to-goal. The methodology uses many random hyper-parameter replicas (64) and reports learning curves/AUC and position-decoding correlations which substantiate the claim that auxiliary tasks improve data efficiency and localisation. Concerns remain about selective reporting (averages over the top-5 runs are emphasized rather than full-distribution statistics), the absence of formal significance testing, and some experimental instability tied to reward clipping; loop-closure training targets also rely on heuristic thresholds (η1, η2) which are not extensively validated. Overall the experimental evidence convincingly supports the central claims, but some reporting and statistical rigor could be improved.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes combining online auxiliary objectives—low-resolution depth prediction and loop-closure detection—with end-to-end A3C training for navigation, and argues for depth-as-an-online loss (classification) rather than input, which is a novel practical angle. It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (e.g., standard A3C baselines, prior supervised auxiliary tasks and the concurrent Jaderberg et al. work) and provides non-trivial engineering and analysis (stacked LSTM routing of velocity/action/reward, depth from LSTM vs conv layer, position-decoder analysis). However, the approach builds on established components (A3C, LSTMs, conv encoders) and is an incremental but meaningful extension rather than a radical new paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (introduction, method, experiments, analysis, conclusion) and states its contributions early; architectural details, training hyperparameters, and ablations are described and many results/diagnostics (position decoding, t-SNE analyses) are explained. Notwithstanding, the provided text contains duplicated/garbled passages and many figure/table references that are not visible here, and some implementation choices (e.g., specific hyperparameter ranges, reward-clipping variants) are deferred to appendices which slightly hinders immediacy of understanding. Overall the exposition is clear enough to follow the methodology and reproduce the main ideas, but polishing and removal of duplicated text would improve readability.
